figures.gloss: The central ritual of modern Heathenry, involving the consecration and offering of mead, ale, or food to the gods, ancestors, or land spirits. It structurally enacts the ancient Germanic concept of 'do ut des'—a gift for a gift.
figures.facet: It categorically rejects Christian models of atonement or submission. The offering is a mechanism of hospitality and mutual obligation among equals or kin, building a shared 'luck' economy.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from medieval sagas (e.g., Heimskringla, Hakon the Good) and codified in the 1970s by Sveinbjörn Beinteinsson and the Ásatrúarfélagið.

figures.gloss: The interconnected web of causality and destiny. Wyrd represents the grand, unfolding tapestry of the cosmos, while Örlög constitutes the specific, accumulated layers of past actions—both ancestral and personal—that shape present conditions.
figures.facet: Unlike passive fatalism, it is highly active. A Heathen is not merely bound by the past; their conscious deeds in the present weave new layers into the well, actively reshaping future Örlög.
figures.provenance: Old English poetry (Beowulf, The Wanderer) and Old Norse eddas (Völuspá); popularized as a central theological tenet by Paul Bauschatz's 1982 work 'The Well and the Tree'.

figures.gloss: A formalized ritual drinking round consisting of toasts, boasts, and oaths spoken over a horn of mead. Typically divided into rounds honoring deities, ancestors, and personal achievements.
figures.facet: Words spoken over the horn are believed to directly alter reality, dropping into the well of Wyrd. A broken oath at sumbel is viewed not just as a social faux pas, but a metaphysical injury.
figures.provenance: Beowulf, Lokasenna, and various sagas; structured into three specific rounds by modern American Heathen groups in the 1970s and 1980s.

figures.gloss: A list of nine ethical maxims—Courage, Truth, Honor, Fidelity, Discipline, Hospitality, Industriousness, Self-Reliance, and Perseverance—intended to summarize a pre-Christian Germanic worldview for modern practitioners.
figures.facet: It highlights the modern craving for systematized morality. Highly controversial today, it is defended by some as a necessary framework, while criticized by strict reconstructionists as a Victorian or overly Protestant codification.
figures.provenance: Created in the 1970s by John Yeowell of the Odinic Rite and Edred Thorsson (Stephen Flowers) of the Asatru Free Assembly.

figures.gloss: The wights or spirits inherent to the natural landscape, rocks, rivers, and trees. They are the immediate, local guardians of the physical environment, demanding respect from anyone who treads or builds on their land.
figures.facet: A modern Heathen might have a distant relationship with the major gods but maintains strict, regular ritual diplomacy with their backyard land-wight to ensure peace and agricultural or domestic success.
figures.provenance: Landnámabók (the Book of Settlements) and Egil's Saga; central to Icelandic law prohibiting dragon-prows from frightening local spirits.

figures.gloss: The Allfather, god of poetry, frenzy, runes, and esoteric wisdom. Modern Heathenry emphasizes his role as a relentless wanderer who sacrifices himself to himself on the World Tree to grasp the secrets of existence.
figures.facet: He is venerated less as an omnipotent, benevolent patriarch and more as a model of relentless self-overcoming and dangerous spiritual initiation. His wisdom is deeply tied to ecstasy and boundary-breaking.
figures.provenance: The Poetic Edda (Hávamál, Völuspá) and Prose Edda; heavily emphasized by Odinic and runic revivalists of the 20th century.

figures.gloss: The red-bearded god of thunder, agriculture, and the protection of humanity. He is the warder of Midgard, actively wielding his hammer Mjölnir against chaotic forces that threaten the cosmic or social order.
figures.facet: He is the democratic, accessible counterweight to Odin's aristocratic elitism. Modern practitioners invoke him for everyday physical strength, defense of the marginalized, and the blessing of marriages and homes.
figures.provenance: The Poetic Edda (Þrymskviða) and widespread archaeological presence of Mjölnir amulets across the Germanic world, adopted today as the primary symbol of the faith.

figures.gloss: Vanir goddess of love, gold, war-dead (she chooses first from the battle-slain for her hall Fólkvangr, Odin the rest), and of seiðr, the magic she is said in Ynglinga saga to have taught Odin himself. Among the most actively venerated deities in contemporary practice, often the central devotional focus for practitioners of reconstructed seiðr.
figures.facet: She, not Odin, is myth's own named source of seiðr: modern Heathenry's oracular revival therefore runs doctrinally through a Vanir goddess rather than the Æsir Allfather, a detail practitioner literature is careful to preserve.
figures.provenance: Snorri Sturluson, Prose Edda (Gylfaginning, Ynglinga saga); the Poetic Edda (Þrymskviða, Völuspá); modern practice documented in Jenny Blain, 'Nine Worlds of Seid-Magic' (2002).

figures.gloss: Vanir god of fertility, sacral kingship, sunshine and rain, and frith (peace); the third of the great blót-receiving gods named beside Odin and Thor in Adam of Bremen's account of the Uppsala temple. Modern kindreds keep his harvest blessings and marriage rites central, and his ship Skíðblaðnir and boar Gullinbursti recur in reconstructed lore.
figures.facet: He is the tradition's clearest sacral-kingship figure without a war portfolio: unlike Odin or Thor, Freyr's cult ties legitimate rule directly to fertility and peace rather than to force, a distinct model of authority modern practitioners invoke for weddings and harvest rites specifically.
figures.provenance: Adam of Bremen, 'Gesta Hammaburgensis' (on the Uppsala temple); Snorri, Prose Edda and Ynglinga saga; the Poetic Edda (Skírnismál); modern harvest-blót practice in Ásatrúarfélagið and American kindred literature.

figures.gloss: Odin's wife, goddess of the hearth, marriage, and foreknowledge — she is said to know all fates but speaks none of them. Mother of Baldr, whose death she tries and fails to forestall by extracting an oath from all things save the mistletoe. Invoked in modern domestic, marriage, and protective ritual as the counterweight to the roster's otherwise all-male divine pair.
figures.facet: Her foreknowledge is total but silent: Frigg is the tradition's one figure who holds complete knowledge of wyrd's unfolding yet, by her own restraint, never intervenes to alter it beyond a single failed attempt — foresight without the will to overrule fate.
figures.provenance: Snorri, Prose Edda (Gylfaginning); the Poetic Edda (Grímnismál, Lokasenna); modern hearth-and-household practice discussed in Diana Paxson's and the Troth's practitioner literature.

figures.gloss: Blood-brother to Odin, shape-shifting instigator of the gods' best schemes and worst disasters, bound beneath the earth after engineering Baldr's death until he breaks free to lead the forces of Ragnarök. In contemporary Heathenry his name marks the tradition's deepest live cultic fault line: 'Lokeans' honor him devotedly while many kindreds forbid his name at sumbel.
figures.facet: The controversy is the theology: unlike every other figure in this roster, what Loki IS to modern Heathenry is itself unsettled and contested — practitioners disagree not on how to honor him but on whether he may be honored at all, making him the tradition's live edge rather than its settled center.
figures.provenance: The Poetic Edda (Lokasenna, Völuspá); Snorri, Prose Edda; the folkish/Lokean divide documented in Jennifer Snook, 'American Heathens' (2015) and practitioner forum literature.

figures.gloss: Oracular trance magic in which a seeress (völva), seated on a raised platform, chants herself and her helpers into an altered state and answers the community's questions about the coming year — rebuilt chiefly from the Greenland seeress scene in Eiríks saga rauða. Modern reconstruction, led from the 1980s-90s by groups such as Hrafnar under Diana Paxson, treats it as Heathenry's principal magical revival alongside runework.
figures.facet: The reconstruction runs from a single vivid scene, not a manual: Eiríks saga's one detailed account of a Greenlandic völva's ritual has been extrapolated by modern practitioners into a repeatable communal rite, making seiðr's revival an unusually visible case of thin source material built out into full practice.
figures.provenance: Eiríks saga rauða, ch. 4 (the Þorbjörg lítilvölva scene); Neil Price, 'The Viking Way' (2002), the standard scholarly study of seiðr; Jenny Blain, 'Nine Worlds of Seid-Magic' (2002) on the modern revival; Diana Paxson, 'Trance-Portation' (2008).

figures.gloss: The Codex Regius corpus of Old Norse mythological and heroic poems — Völuspá, Hávamál, Þrymskviða, and others — compiled in 13th-century Iceland from older oral material. Every deity and rite in this roster traces to it or to Snorri's related Prose Edda; modern Heathenry reads it as primary lore and moral touchstone rather than as revealed scripture.
figures.facet: The corpus's Christian-era compilation is treated as a feature, not a flaw, of its authority: because no claim of revelation is made for it, modern practitioners can debate and reinterpret the Edda's poems as inherited literature — lore to be read, not doctrine to be obeyed — a stance the tradition states explicitly against comparison to scriptural religions.
figures.provenance: The Codex Regius manuscript (GKS 2365 4to, c. 1270); standard modern edition/translation: Carolyne Larrington, 'The Poetic Edda' (Oxford, 1996; rev. 2014).

figures.gloss: Icelandic farmer, poet, and reciter of rímur (1924-1993) who co-founded the Ásatrúarfélagið at Hótel Borg on the first day of summer, 1972, and served as its first allsherjargoði; the organization won official state recognition as a religious body in 1973, permitting it to perform legally binding ceremonies. He led the first public outdoor blót held in Iceland since public sacrifice was outlawed in the year 1000.
figures.facet: A single farmer-poet's petition reopened a religion the state itself had suppressed a thousand years earlier: Sveinbjörn's achievement was bureaucratic as much as spiritual, converting a private revival into a legally equal faith — the founding act modern Heathenry measures its own legitimacy against.
figures.provenance: Ásatrúarfélagið organizational history; obituaries and retrospectives (e.g. Jason Pitzl-Waters, 'Forty Years of Ásatrúarfélagið', The Wild Hunt, 2012); Wikipedia and Ásatrúarfélagið primary documentation of the 1972-73 founding and recognition.

figures.gloss: A joint statement, first circulated in August 2016 and named for Hávamál stanza 127 ('when you see misdeeds, speak out against them, and give your enemies no frith'), denouncing the Ásatrú Folk Assembly's explicit exclusion of non-white and LGBT Heathens and defining Heathen membership by practice and deed rather than ancestry. Ultimately signed by roughly 180 organizations across more than 20 countries; stewardship passed to the inclusive umbrella group the Troth in 2024.
figures.facet: The schism is fought on the tradition's own turf: rather than appealing to outside civil-rights language, Declaration 127 draws its authority from a stanza of the Hávamál itself, making the folkish/universalist fault line a dispute over correct reading of shared scripture rather than an import from outside the religion.
figures.provenance: Declaration127.com; The Troth's published history of the declaration and its 2024 transfer of stewardship; contemporary coverage in The Wild Hunt and Patheos's 'Agora' Pagan blog (2016).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Blót establishes the relationship with the divine through physical sacrifice, while Sumbel solidifies the social and spiritual bonds of the community through sacred speech.
Intra-tradition: Words spoken into the horn during Sumbel are explicitly believed to drop into the well of Örlög, permanently weaving the speaker's oaths into the web of Wyrd.
Intra-tradition: They form a complementary dyad of the aristocratic, esoteric seeker (Odin) and the pragmatic, accessible guardian of the common folk (Thor).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Odinism bridges the practitioner to the ancient mythic axis; Odin's ritual sacrifice on Yggdrasil serves as the ultimate template for shamanic initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Heathenry directly revitalizes the historic Norse Thor, shifting him from a mythic relic to a living patron of the working class and community defense.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a fundamental, underlying cosmic matrix of cause, effect, and truthful action that even deities are subject to.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying cosmic and moral order that humans must actively maintain through correct speech, ritual, and harmonious living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are wandering, gray-bearded archetypes of esoteric knowledge who wield profound magical power through poetry and song rather than brute force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A classic Dumézilian parallel where both are sovereign, magically-binding sky gods whose authority rests on occult knowledge and fetters rather than martial combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are Indo-European thunderers associated with a specific symbolic weapon (hammer and wheel) and invoked for the protection of the tribe.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions venerate localized, territorial nature spirits that directly control the fertility and safety of the land, requiring active appeasement by local inhabitants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both integrate the veneration of the active, mighty dead directly into living community ritual, relying on spoken lineage and toasts or offerings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature outdoor, pragmatic rituals centered on offering a sacred food or drink directly to deities and natural forces to secure agricultural and communal luck.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both revolve around cyclical community feasts where humans and gods share the yield of the earth, reinforcing cosmic order through reciprocal hospitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent attempts to codify and systematize an indigenous polytheistic worldview into a concrete framework for correct behavior and ritual procedure.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: communal open-air offering rites rebuilt from medieval sources and archaeology on the same gift-for-a-gift logic, in movements that arose independently but in parallel.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: each movement's modern systematization of an order-concept from old sources — darna as harmony to be maintained, wyrd as the web one weaves into — parallel constructions, no contact required.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from period sources on the reciprocal gift-logic — libation and khernips beside mead and horn — parallel modern reconstructions, not contact.

Class seam of modern reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from ancient sources on a reciprocal logic — senut's reversion of offerings beside the blót's gift-for-a-gift — parallel modern constructions from unrelated ancient models.
Unrelated traditions converge on the same idea: a weave of fate that binds even the highest gods, spun or measured rather than decreed.
Vanir siblings, hostages exchanged to the Æsir together in the Vanir-Æsir war and worshipped as a linked pair of fertility, love, and peace deities beside the war-oriented Æsir gods.
Ynglinga saga names Freyja as the one who taught seiðr to the Æsir, including Odin himself — the modern oracular revival's mythic point of origin is her, not the Allfather.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: modern Heathenry's devotional Frigg directly revitalizes the historic Norse Frigg of the eddas, shifting her from mythic Allmother to a living patroness of hearth, marriage, and household protection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same bound trickster of the eddas, now the object of modern Heathenry's sharpest live cultic dispute — devoted Lokean veneration against outright prohibition at sumbel.

Sworn blood-brothers in the lore (Lokasenna's claim), whose modern-practice relationship is inverted from the myth: Odin is honored without controversy while Loki's honoring is the tradition's open question.
Hávamál is framed within the corpus as Odin's own gnomic sayings; the Poetic Edda is not merely a source about Odin but, in its central wisdom-poem, presented as spoken in his voice.
Sveinbjörn led the first public outdoor blót held in Iceland since the year 1000, personally reopening the very rite this roster's own Blót entry describes as the tradition's central ritual.

Both modern revivals ground their theology in a single core inherited text read as primary lore rather than revealed scripture — the Codex Regius's Christian-era compilation of pre-Christian poems beside Sallustius's pagan-authored catechism — though the two texts differ sharply in origin, one folklore transcription, the other philosophical treatise.

Class seam of 20th-century polytheist reconstruction: two founding organizers, a generation apart, who each built the durable institutional vehicle (Ásatrúarfélagið 1972; YSEE 1997) that carried a suppressed pre-Christian religion into state-recognized modern practice.

Medieval Norse sources themselves link seiðr and Sámi noaidi practice through recorded contact and intermarriage of magic-workers in the north (e.g. Vatnsdæla saga); the connection is attested historically, not merely a modern structural echo, though the exact direction of influence is debated by scholars such as Neil Price.

Both are 20th-century reconstructions of an oracular/journeying trance technique built out from thin historical or ethnographic source material into a repeatable modern group practice — parallel revivalist method, unrelated lineages.

Class seam of modern reconstructionism: both adopt a short numbered list of ethical maxims as a portable moral code for a revived polytheism — the Delphic Maxims genuinely ancient and inscribed, the Nine Noble Virtues a 1970s codification from saga material — a shared move, different degrees of ancient authorship.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: both name the same reciprocal gift-logic as their offerings' explicit theological mechanism — kharis built through libation, the blót's 'do ut des' built through mead and food — parallel modern articulations of an ancient reciprocity idea.
modern Heathenry revives Óðinn as a living cultic figure, invoked by the same names and myths as the Allfather who gathers the slain and hangs for wisdom.
modern Heathenry's Wyrd and Örlög directly revive the Norns' web of fate as a lived ethical-cosmological concept, not merely a mythic memory.

Flowers, a runologist, derives 'Runa' directly from the Proto-Germanic root behind 'rune' and the mystery Odin wins through self-sacrifice on the world-tree in Hávamál — a modern Word deliberately built from an existing Germanic mythic source, not an independent coincidence.

dossier.essence: The modern revival and reconstruction of the pre-Christian Germanic and Norse religion, emerging in the early 1970s — organized in Iceland as the Ásatrúarfélagið (founded 1972, its first allsherjargoði the poet Sveinbjörn Beinteinsson) and in Britain and North America under names such as Ásatrú, Odinism, and Heathenry. Practitioners worship the Norse gods — the Æsir and Vanir, including Odin, Thor, and Freyja — honor ancestors and land-spirits, and rebuild their religion from medieval texts and archaeology.
dossier.cosmos: Drawn from Norse myth as preserved in the Eddas: nine worlds arrayed on the world-tree Yggdrasil, from Asgard of the gods through Midgard of humans to Hel of the dead, framed between the shaping of the world from the giant Ymir and its destruction and renewal at Ragnarök. Fate is central — the web of wyrd and örlög laid down at the well of the Norns, which even the gods must meet, and which many modern Heathens hold is actively re-woven by present deeds. Afterlife beliefs (Valhalla, Hel, rebirth in the family line) are various and loosely held.
dossier.practice: The two core rites are the blót — an offering of mead, ale, or food to gods, ancestors, and land-wights on the reciprocal principle of 'a gift for a gift' — and the sumbel, a ritual round of toasts, boasts, and oaths over the horn whose spoken words are held to drop into the well of wyrd. Practice is decentralized among households, kindreds, and larger fellowships, led by elected or informal goðar rather than a priesthood; widely cited values include hospitality, kin-peace (frith), and the modern, contested 'Nine Noble Virtues.'
dossier.canon: No scripture; the sources are the medieval Icelandic texts — the 'Poetic Edda' (especially Völuspá and Hávamál), Snorri Sturluson's 'Prose Edda' (c. 1220) and 'Heimskringla,' the Icelandic sagas, and Old English poetry such as 'Beowulf' — read alongside archaeology and place-name evidence.
dossier.reflects: The reconstructionist impulse — a religion deliberately rebuilt, and honest that its own scriptures were written down by Christians centuries after conversion — foregrounding reciprocity and kin-bound honor over salvation or submission.
sources: Jennifer Snook, 'American Heathens: The Politics of Identity in a Pagan Religious Movement' (2015)
Stefanie von Schnurbein, 'Norse Revival: Transformations of Germanic Neopaganism' (2016)
Michael F. Strmiska (ed.), 'Modern Paganism in World Cultures' (2005)
Jeffrey Kaplan, 'Radical Religion in America: Millenarian Movements from the Far Right to the Children of Noah' (1997)
